title: A Beehive of Activity
summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program on the University of Florida campus in Gainesville on September 14, 2011, Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons recalls her first impressions of the Atlanta SNCC office as a young Spelman student: a chaotic, untidy beehive with James Forman at its center, Ruby Doris Smith Robinson a fixture, and Black, white, and Japanese American staff she met for the first time.

title: Breaking Spelman's Prohibition
summary: Simmons describes Ella Baker's gentle, unobtrusive teaching at SNCC gatherings, and the terrible tension of slipping to the office while violating Spelman's prohibition on student political activity, knowing that as the first in her family to reach college on a full scholarship she could be sent home, with her parents refusing to back her up if she got involved.

title: Pulled Asunder
summary: She recounts the figures who pulled her toward engagement, Staughton Lynd teaching the long Black freedom struggle, Howard Zinn as a radical force, and Vincent Harding and his wife at the Mennonite House Sunday dinners, set against her own folks' warnings, leaving her feeling pulled asunder, with James Forman as an early mentor and Ruby Doris a Spelman dropout she could relate to but feared becoming.

title: The Worst of the Worst
topic: Heroes and the Fear of Mississippi
summary: Simmons remembers the Nashville staff and the Mississippi field workers, including women, whose stories of being jailed and beaten blew her away and struck home, John Lewis among the dropouts she befriended, and how her grandmother's warning that Mississippi was the worst of the worst for Black people made that state loom larger than Albany as she wrestled with taking the further plunge.

title: They Will Kill Whites Too
summary: She describes the emotional struggle of choosing to go to Mississippi in 1964, helping plan Freedom Summer through Staughton Lynd's Freedom School curriculum, and her hope that the thousand white students would offer protection, a hope shattered when, during the second week of orientation, James Chaney and two white workers she had just met disappeared and Bob Moses said they were probably dead.
related_events: Murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner

title: Are We There Yet
summary: Assigned to Laurel with James Garrett and Lester McKinney because it was too dangerous to send whites, Simmons recounts believing they would never get out alive but feeling she could not turn back having severed ties with her family, and waking from sleeping the whole drive from Ohio to find that Mississippi, where she half expected monsters in the trees, looked no different from the rest of the country.

part: Waiting on You All My Life
title: I've Been Waiting on You All My Life
summary: Because Laurel had no structure, the team based in well-organized Hattiesburg and drove thirty miles north knocking on doors, and Simmons tells how Mrs. Eberhart Spinks, hearing she was a Freedom Rider, said come in, I've been waiting on you all my life, taking her in and arranging for neighbor Mrs. Carrie Clayton to house the two men, the beginning of the Laurel Project.

title: She Was the Driving Force
topic: Two Women Who Took Them In
summary: Simmons contrasts the two hosts, the older widowed Mrs. Clayton with her lovely home who said little, and the fighter Mrs. Eberhart Spinks who declared she was sick and tired and ready for change, whose husband worked a union job at the Masonite plant and worried for their teenage son, and how once Jackson and Atlanta were alerted some twenty-three volunteers were sent in.

title: You've Got to Step Up
summary: She recounts how Lester McKinney, the seasoned worker, vanished and was found jailed on an old Jones County charge, forced to sign an affidavit not to return for five years, leaving only Simmons and the Californian James Garrett, so that James Forman called and told her to become project director, promising relief that never came as she wound up staying sixteen months.

title: Those Skills Just Came to the Floor
summary: Simmons reflects on how she took charge despite feeling unprepared, drawing on a childhood of leadership as editor of her school paper, president of her high school sorority and Girl Scout troop, and on watching her grandparents in Memphis register voters and Black churches host campaigns, with Mrs. Spinks, active in her church and the NAACP, reminding her of her grandmother.

title: Like a Barn Raising
summary: She describes the volunteers rolling up their sleeves, the first office on Mrs. Clayton's back porch, and renting a wealthy Black man's shuttered nightclub that the community fixed up like a barn raising, only for it to be firebombed; there they ran a Freedom School for grades one through twelve, adult literacy, a Freedom Nursery School, mock voter registration, and organized the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party chapter.

title: Thrown Under the Bus
summary: Simmons recalls preparing the Laurel group for the state convention in Jackson where two local delegates were chosen, and the disappointment of Atlantic City, where naive about the political system they believed they would be seated until President Johnson threw them under the bus to keep the regular Democrats, a profound blow that jaded the seasoned SNCC workers with the lesson that politics was elevated over dying people.

title: We're Just Getting Started
summary: She describes deciding not to leave for Antioch College, where Staughton Lynd had helped her enroll with a recommendation from Coretta Scott King, because she could not abandon Mrs. Spinks, Mrs. Clayton, and Mr. Richardson when the work was just getting started; Antioch graciously held her scholarship until January and two other women, Lynelle Barrett and Marion Davidson, stayed on sharing a bedroom at Mrs. Spinks's home.

title: A Feminist Way of Organizing
topic: Consensus and the Fight for Resources
summary: Simmons reflects on becoming a leader, recognizing in hindsight a feminist, bottom-up style modeled on SNCC's ultra-democratic consensus, where the project discussed everything for hours because all their lives were on the line, and on the struggle as one of only three women project directors in Mississippi that summer to be taken seriously and to fight Jackson for good cars and typewriters the men got first.

title: An Absolute No No
summary: Having grown up under total segregation in Memphis until she met white professors like Staughton Lynd, Simmons describes standing up to white male volunteers her age from Stanford and Yale amid swirling class, race, and gender tensions, and taking an absolute stand against the sexual harassment of women on the project, having herself been sexually assaulted by someone she trusted at the orientation.

title: I Hope They Would Say I Was Fair
summary: Simmons recounts reconnecting with a SNCC volunteer from Stanford who sent her a fragile letter she had written from Laurel, treasured because she has no documents from the period, and says she hopes the volunteers would remember her as fair and dedicated to keeping them and the community people alive and on their property.

part: It Was an Act of God
title: It Blew the Gas Line Away
summary: She describes the period after most volunteers left, when the project shrank to six including two expelled high school students, Ulysses Everett and Ben Hartfield from Hattiesburg, and the illiterate local John Handy, and how their second office, half of a shotgun duplex secured without disclosing who they were, was firebombed, the Molotov cocktail blowing the gas line away from the house in what neighbors called an act of God that let the family next door escape.

title: This Is Our Church
summary: Simmons recounts expanding into desegregating Laurel's restaurants in Klan-heavy Jones County, leading a group of high school students past a sheriff who drew his gun and cursed her, singing freedom songs outside the jail when Ulysses and Ben were arrested, and Mrs. Spinks fighting battles in the Black community, threatening to boycott churches whose scared, displaced ministers wanted to shut the movement out.

title: Something Wrong With You
topic: Trauma and a Move to New York
summary: Simmons tells how a large demonstration at the Jackson Capitol led to roughly a thousand arrests and fifteen brutal days held in livestock houses at the state fairgrounds, after which she caused two head-on car accidents, prompting James Forman to insist something was wrong and bring her to New York to work with the Friends of SNCC, where over three months she discovered the Nation of Islam, black nationalist street preachers in Harlem, and LeRoi Jones's Black Arts Theatre.

title: A Whole Nother Song
summary: After Julian Bond was elected but barred from his seat in the Georgia legislature, James Forman asked Simmons to come to Atlanta to organize his re-election, and she brought her three months of black-nationalist thinking into the SNCC offices, recruiting her future husband Michael Simmons, whose brother had been the Nation's national secretary and roomed with Malcolm X, along with Dwight Williams and an older military veteran, forming the nucleus of the Atlanta Project.

title: Will We Be Accepted Ever
summary: Simmons describes setting up Bond's campaign in Vine City, Atlanta's poorest and most rundown area of shacks beset by poor housing, unemployment, addiction, and bad schools, around the corner from Nation of Islam Mosque Number 15, and how winning his re-election only to face a renewed fight for his seat pushed the group to question whether integration was possible or whether race ran too deep for Black people ever to be accepted as full Americans.

title: A Wollensak Tape Recorder
summary: Simmons explains that the Atlanta Project's discussions of where to go next, recorded on a Wollensak tape recorder and typed up by herself and two other women, became the group-authored position paper on Black Power later credited to Stokely Carmichael, and how Freedom Summer had so changed SNCC's racial ratio that justifying resources to the many white staff at the Atlanta headquarters fed the argument that whites should organize in their own communities.

title: This Catches Fire
topic: Black Power and Division in SNCC
summary: She recalls the project's innovative organizing with a loudspeaker truck and hot dogs, noticing that playing a Malcolm X record drew people out, and the questions spreading through SNCC as Bob Moses's group worked Lowndes County with Stokely and others looked to Africa, until Stokely stood up in Mississippi and said black power, catching fire while Forman lost control and the deeply religious John Lewis opposed it, opening a real division in the organization.

title: SNCC Was Home
topic: The Pain of the White Volunteers
summary: Simmons reflects on the pain of the longtime white volunteers, meeting Bob Zellner years later to discuss the break before a Chicago class and learning that his own Klan family had threatened to kill him so that SNCC was his only home, and recalls the statements against the Vietnam War, which she helped draft, and on Israel and Palestine, drafted by a woman she hopes to interview at the Chicago SNCC reunion, which created a firestorm.

title: Wondering What We Were Questioning
summary: Asked about the new SNCC's vision for the future, Simmons admits no vision had coalesced, that she was questioning what she had believed and wondering whether older strands of Black thought from slavery forward pointed away from integration, recognizing a two-pronged tradition that fought to integrate into the country and that sought instead to leave it or carve out a separate space for African Americans.

title: A Totally Disruptive Element
summary: Simmons describes the shock of the Atlanta Project being expelled from SNCC, ostensibly over a three-thousand-dollar check sent to their address that Forman insisted belonged to SNCC and they refused to turn over, leading to a lawyer and a fight, but underneath because Forman saw the project as a disruptive element pulling the organization off course, and her lasting regret at never reconciling with the mentor she loved before he died.

title: Never Heard the Term Oral History
summary: Simmons recounts going to work for the American Friends Service Committee's project on government surveillance and civil liberties, traveling the country to interview members of the American Indian Movement, Brown Berets, Black Panther Party, and Corky Gonzales's group about COINTELPRO, gathering their FBI files under the Freedom of Information Act, with staff like Ken Lawrence documenting the Mississippi State Sovereignty Commission, the Quakers having launched the project after finding infiltrators in their meetings.

title: The People We Thought Would Protect Us
summary: She describes learning that Mississippi FBI agents fed information to the Sovereignty Commission and possibly the Klan, that Fred Hampton's bodyguard had drugged the apartment so no shots were fired in what was an assassination by Chicago police and the FBI, and that California Panthers and members of US were pitted against one another by agents provocateurs, a shock given how openly and trustingly the Laurel project had once talked to the agents who came around.

title: A Mountain of Skulls
summary: Asked about the long-term effects of trauma, Simmons says she stayed too busy moving from movement to movement to feel damaged, but that working with students who cannot believe such things happened, and being among the first Americans flown into Cambodia after the fall of Pol Pot to see a mountain of skulls and hear survivors' oral histories in Phnom Penh, taught her that cruelty is a human problem requiring change in people's hearts as well as in law.

title: The Only Way That Leaves People
summary: Simmons explains how her movement into Sufism, the mystical stream in Islam, brought her full circle to understand and re-embrace Dr. King's message of nonviolence, which she had once rejected, as the only real way to bring about deep change that does not leave corpses piled mountains high or wounded spirits and souls, however difficult that path may be.

title: When People Ran, I Ran
summary: She recalls the dire despair of Dr. King's assassination, learning of it at a Manhattan hotel during a National Council of Negro Women meeting, dropping her bags and taking the subway up to Harlem where she joined a riot, running when others ran amid fires, needing to vent, and carrying for years afterward an anger and a belief that the government had probably had him killed.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

title: A School Where We Learn
topic: Seventeen Years With a Sufi Teacher
summary: Simmons closes on meeting a Sufi mystic from Sri Lanka in 1971 and spending seventeen years studying with the teacher Bawa Muhaiyaddeen, gradually embracing the teaching that life is a school for learning to choose good and reject bad, which led her to reflect anew on Dr. King and her Christian upbringing and to re-embrace nonviolence as the only way to bring about lasting social change, grateful her story will outlast her.
